speaker: interviewee
text: [BEEP]

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape 4. The date is January 16, 1997. I'm conducting an interview with Barbara Marlow. The location is London, England.

speaker: interviewee
text: What I think about hiding in Warsaw, the period '42 to '44. Although I was never actually living with my mother for any length of time, yes, we did spend the occasional night at Mrs. Wedecka's. And that was one of the hairy experiences we had because on that occasion, her daughter was conducting a meeting in one room. It was early in the morning. My mother and I were sleeping in a big double bed in another bedroom when there was banging on the door. And in those days, there was a certain way of banging on the door which bode ill for anybody inside. And we heard these men at the door. Mrs. Wedecka's apartment was a single story in the courtyard of a big apartment block. My mother jumped out of bed and through the open window onto the flat roof behind and hid between the two windows while I stayed in bed. I don't know why I stayed in bed, why I didn't do anything. In any case, I heard the voices.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at the last minute, I put my head under the pillow while-- when my mother jumped out of bed, she had thrown the eiderdown to one side, which hid me. And it was obviously somebody looking for easy money because he came in and said to Mrs. Wedecka, and who slept in this bed? And she said, I did.

speaker: interviewee
text: At the same time, she must have been wondering where we were because she knew that that was our bed. In any case, he left. He never found us. And that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who, who was he? Did you find out from Mrs. Wedecka?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She didn't know who it was. It was somebody-- it could, it could have been unconnected with us. It could have been somebody-- as I said, there was this meeting with several young girls in another room. And that was explained somehow. But you see, this sort of thing also happened. There were no schools in Warsaw, no secondary schools, as I've said before. So children used to learn in groups with teachers from the schools coming to give them different courses. Now, that was forbidden. You could be arrested for carrying a schoolbook in the street. So we learned an awful lot by heart. You had one book at the lesson, and you learned from that. Good training, but-- so I couldn't say that that was the occasion that they came for us. It could have been all sorts of other reasons.

speaker: interviewer
text: But nevertheless, very frightening?

speaker: interviewee
text: But nevertheless, it was frightening.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what your mother said when she climbed back in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she was, she was petrified. Said, how could, how could I have left you behind? It was just instinct.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was another occasion. It was just before Christmas '43. We had met, because that was the way we would meet sometimes and go for a walk in the evening when it was getting dark before the police hour. And we stopped to get a newspaper. And the newspaper vendor, a woman, turned around and threw her arms around my mother and said, oh, madame. I'm so happy to see you. How is the master and the young master? You know, who are you? She said, oh, I used to bring the Sunday papers to your apartment before the war. Well, obviously, she knew we were Jewish. She knew the name. And all we had was kindness from her that she was so pleased to see us. So-- but that, that was life, you know. Could have met somebody who didn't like you, an old maid who didn't leave on very good terms, anything like that could happen.

speaker: interviewee
text: But all the people who had worked both for my parents and my grandparents helped us throughout the war in every way they could. So that was sort of the 1942, '44 period. And then in December of '44, we saw the war was ending. The Russians were getting nearer. We were just waiting to be liberated by the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you felt that Germany was going to lose by that stage? Were you getting that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Oh, by that time, we knew Germany was going to lose. After Stalingrad, we always heard about the trains bringing the wounded back. There were masses of trains coming back from Russia with soldiers, amputees, and so on. By that time, it was clear the Germans were going to lose. But they were getting quite frantic. And they were getting very, very nasty. In 1944, early in the year, they started taking hostages because the underground was killing German soldiers. And Germans who were particularly cruel, they started taking hostages and conducting public executions on the streets of Warsaw. I witnessed one of the executions. I mean, first, there were the roundups. They would close both ends of the street, whatever street they had designated, and just take everybody away. And then there would be notices on-- plastered all over the city giving the names of those who would be executed. Some would be sent to Germany. Some would be kept, generally, young men or people, intelligentsia, whom they would shoot. And they would say, all right, the next German soldier or German national killed, 20 or 50 or whatever number people will be executed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about the execution that you saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was on my way to a Girl Guide meeting when they closed the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know which street it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was near [NON-ENGLISH]. And they just-- there was a van. And at, at the beginning, they didn't gag the people to be executed. But then they started crying out, long live Poland, and so on and so forth. And of course, the Germans didn't like it. So they gagged them. They just lined them up against a wall and machine-gunned them. And then they-- again, they would take the bodies away so that they couldn't get a martyr's funeral or-- but it was quite amazing. Within minutes of the Germans leaving, the place would be absolutely covered in flowers, with candles. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you saw that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was martyrs' blood.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that affect you as a, what, a 14-year-old as you would have been then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I told you. This was something which was every day. And yes, I had a very-- I wanted to live. But at the same time, I, I accepted the fact that I could die at any moment. It, it was just one of those things which, which happened. There was no security or certainty of survival. Just one of those things.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I think we're also immune to feeling in terms of death. I've seen so many. You know, one death is one thing. But as I said, I saw people dead in the ghetto. I saw people shot. It just happened. It could happen to me. It could happen to, to anybody else.

speaker: interviewee
text: My best friend from the day I was born, virtually, was a year, a year and a day older than I. She never went to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Inka Szpet. Her father was a director of one of the banks. And he was arrested by the Germans at the beginning of the war for acting against the interest of the German state and put on trial. Although he was Jewish, he was put on trial and sentenced. Don't know what happened to him. And she and her mother stayed on the Aryan side. And sadly, her mother was working also in the underground while she, her grandmother, her aunt with two little boys, who looked like cherubs with golden-- curly, golden hair. Her aunt's husband had been killed by the Russians in Katyn. And they were hiding in the mountains, in some village in the Carpathian mountains, while her mother was in Warsaw. And her mother went to see them, and she was followed. And the whole family was executed. I knew about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know how come my mother found out, but I knew. So these things just, just happened. And when you are exposed to death all the time, in a way it loses all meaning. You take-- you accept it. You, you want to stay alive. Of course I wanted to stay alive. But that was just one of those things.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were 14. At that stage, a girl starts to change and comes into puberty. How did the war and what was going on around you affect those things, those changes in you as a young woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at that time, I was 13 and a half. In fact, I hadn't started menstruating till much later. I think that was probably also the result of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the stress.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the stress and everything else. I don't-- again, it was different. We were all so-- contact with the boys in the underground were, were colleagues. We were comrades. I don't-- I was too young. Or I hadn't matured enough. So that was spring/summer '44. I knew we were preparing for the Warsaw rising. I was too-- I was one of the youngest ones in our, in the group. So we were not given any special assignments for the Warsaw rising. And we just-- before it started, we were transporting field dressings and so on to the designated first aid posts and things like that. But on the day the Warsaw rising was to start, I knew it was going to start. I was told it was starting. And I went to see my mother, who at that time was-- she was still living with Mrs. Nowodworska, but I was living with another family in the same street. It was quite a long street facing the [NON-ENGLISH] garden, [NON-ENGLISH]. And my mother lived at one end. I lived at the other end.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to see her, and I said-- to tell her that the rising was going to start. Walking along the street, there was a German unit walking. And I saw an armband of the Polish Home Army. It was a red and white armband, Polish national colors, with a stamped PW, which was Fighting Poland. That was the emblem. So I dropped a handkerchief over it and collected that. I went to see my mother, and I said to her, do you want me to stay? And she said, no. It's all right. You go back. You're so close. If anything happens, we'll get together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she didn't know that what happened was that we were completely cut off. That part where she was was in German hands, while where I was was in Polish hands. And I had no idea what had happened to her. I couldn't get to her. I had no communication. It was only four months later that I found out what had happened to her. But at that time, as far as I knew, she might have been dead. And I did some work as a messenger for the Polish units. Then, I worked with a unit which was sort of a social service unit. We used to take whatever food there was to hospitals and for babies, powdered milk, and moved around. On one-- on the 1st of September, I met my brother's friend, who had come from the old city, which had fallen to the Germans, who told me that his brother had been killed and that he was going to another part of Warsaw with the remains of his unit. And said, we are the only two left. Because he thought that his parents had been killed, but they hadn't. I thought my mother was dead. And he suggested that I join their unit. I didn't want to leave my friends, so that was the last time I saw him. He died a few days later. And so I think about the second week of September, I was in a part of Warsaw which was captured by the German where we were all lined up and taken to a transit camp outside Warsaw, might be about 30 kilometers from Warsaw, called Pruszkow, which were old railway works and sidings, which the Germans used for the population of Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was with you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: But no, you said 'we were rounded up.' Who was the--

speaker: interviewee
text: All the people in the area. I was on my own. I had just gone over there with a message and that was it. I was on my own.

speaker: interviewer
text: So no one you knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We had-- they made us walk to the camp. We went through an area-- some fields just outside Warsaw. There were allotments and so on. And it was the first time I had seen any fresh fruit and vegetables for weeks. Well, since the first [INAUDIBLE]. Because there was no fresh food in Warsaw during that time. We used to eat what we called spit soup, which was oats, but not property cleaned, just boiled up. So the shortage of food was absolutely appalling. You couldn't bake bread. You had no fuel. There was no electricity. There was a shortage of water. You had to go to standpipes in the street. And if you were in an area where there were either Germans shooting from the rooftops, many, many people were killed while queuing for water. So it was pretty hairy. I was wearing just what I had worn for six weeks of the Warsaw rising. No change of clothes at that stage. And my shoes were completely cut. I wore a new pair of shoes, but they were literally cut into strips by all the debris and the ruins we had to walk over during that time. And I had a sort of anorak. When I saw the first tomatoes, I picked all the tomatoes I could in sight and stuffed them inside the lining of my anorak, which didn't do it much good, I can tell you. And got to Pruszkow. And we were in [NON-ENGLISH] barracks, or whatever they were-- huts. And everybody was saying a different story what they were going to do with us, deporting us to concentration camps, deporting us to Germany for labor, all sorts of stories going around. I cut my hair then because I hadn't been able to wash it for six weeks, and I had long plaits, which I couldn't even undo. They were so full of dust and debris. So I cut my hair with somebody's knife. And the next day, there was a selection. We were all lined up. And it was, again, a lottery. You didn't know where you wanted to be. See, I was trying to work out what was the best thing to do. Old people, young people. I didn't realize at that time, of course, that old people would-- were being killed. So I said, well, I would do better with a group of young people-- old people and children rather than the younger ones who might be going to Germany for labor. And although I don't speak German-- I know a few words-- when I came, there were two Germans with a stick pushing you one way or the other. I just stood my ground and I said, I'm, I'm only 14. Because I was tall. And he was still pushing me to the group of young people. And as I said, at that point, I couldn't have cared less what happened. I just run across to the other group.

speaker: interviewer
text: With the old people and the children.

speaker: interviewee
text: With the older people and the children. I know it flashed through my mind, he may shoot. And I said, [INAUDIBLE]. And then we were taken to the sidings and loaded onto open cattle trucks. And again, we didn't know where we were going.

speaker: interviewer
text: The train started. And we're looking through the cracks. And people were climbing on top of each other to see where we were going, reading station names, and saying, oh, we are going west. And then, speculation, what we were going to-- where are we going?

speaker: interviewee
text: And people along the route would throw us food, apples and bread. Because we-- the Germans didn't give us anything. We had no food. And we had no water or anything. And every so often, the train would stop on the sidings. And there were a couple of other young people on the train with me. And we started saying, well, we're going towards Germany. It's not a good idea. I saw the name of a station, which I remembered that this old governess of my friend [NON-ENGLISH] had gone to work there as a housekeeper on an estate nearby and also that the daughter of that family was with me in the Warsaw rising. She was older than I. I said, all right. That's where I am going to head for.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know how far from Warsaw that was, the name of this place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you on, on the cattle truck?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it kept stopping and starting. It wasn't very far. It was probably 80 miles from Warsaw, a bit more, perhaps. In any case, that night when the train stopped, we decided we were going to jump off. And the people who were with us didn't want us to do it. They said, oh, you-- they will shoot you. So we said, OK. We take the risk. Well, if they don't shoot you, they may shoot us. Well, in any case, nobody shot us. Don't think anybody shot them. And we were out in the country. Now, the problem was that the Germans were letting quite a few people from Warsaw into the countryside. And all the villages in reasonable proximity to Warsaw were full of refugees. So the peasants weren't very keen to have any more. If you came, sort of freelance, they didn't want to know. Go away. So I started making my way to this area, which I thought I could find a home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you alone?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was alone then. I sort of hitchhiked with-- on peasants' carts, walked. It was quite difficult because I had horrible boils on my legs from lack of fresh fruit and vegetables.

speaker: interviewer
text: In one village, in the village shop, the woman asked me whether I could read and write. I said, yes. She said, well, do you want a job? I said, great. I'll give you a note to my daughter. And she wrote literally, take her in. She will teach the kids. And it was in terrible Polish, had awful spelling. But armed with this note, I went off to this farm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, nobody wanted me to teach the children. They decided the best thing I could do if I wanted to earn my keep was to take the cows to pasture. I've never met a cow before. So I was the butt of the children's sharp tongues and good ideas and advised to ride the cow if I wanted her to go in a certain direction. I lost my job, to cut a long story short. I used the whip on the kids, and I lost my job. But finally, I arrived at the other place where, where I was heading for. And I was taken in and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of that place?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]. And I was put to bed to recuperate.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they recognized you and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, a housekeeper knew me and knew my mother and knew the family she had lived with, so-- but again, they had so many friends, acquaintances. You see, anybody who knew anybody outside Warsaw was heading that way when they left Warsaw. So all the manor houses and estates were full of refugees. So they said, OK. When I had recuperated, they decided to send me on.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they treat your legs? I mean, boils like that would have needed treatment. You said that when you were better--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they just healed. I was eating normally, and they healed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: In those days, there were different times and different expectations. And they, they contacted Mrs. Nowodworska's aunt, who had an estate called Sekursko near [NON-ENGLISH]. And she said, yes. I could come over. And that's where I found out what-- well, what had happened to my mother up to a point. Because when I arrived there, I met Mrs. Nowodworska's eldest daughter and her-- with her younger sister, who told us, me that, in fact, beginning of August, the Germans took all the inhabitants of the houses there as protection for the tanks, German tanks, advancing against Polish positions. There were women, children, regardless. And my mother was among them. Fortunately, there were-- the Polish unit liberated them from the Germans, but she found herself in the old city, which was quite a way from where we were. And where the, for a month, the fighting was absolutely ferocious. So all they knew was that she had been there and that the last thing that happened, she had gone to the camp at Pruszkow as well. But what had happened to her after that, again, no one knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. A place to stop. We're finding some very good--
